findings.description: N+1 query: per-product COUNT(*) on movements inside the loop turns one report into K+1 queries (each a likely full scan of movements) for K low-stock SKUs — replace with a single LEFT JOIN ... GROUP BY aggregate.

Unbounded SELECT + fetchall() materializes the entire products table only to filter stock <= reorder_level in Python at line 81 — push the predicate into the SQL WHERE clause so only low-stock rows are fetched.

The 30-day COUNT predicate on (sku, created_at) almost certainly lacks a covering index (schema not in scope), making each aggregate a movements table scan — add a composite index on movements(sku, created_at).

search_by_name uses leading-wildcard LIKE '%...%' (unindexable, full table scan per search) with no LIMIT, so a short fragment materializes essentially the whole catalog into Product objects — add LIMIT/pagination and consider FTS5 for substring search.

Every method opens and closes a fresh sqlite3 connection with default journal mode (no reuse/pooling, no WAL, no pragmas), paying connection setup plus a journal fsync per operation in a pipeline hot path — reuse a long-lived connection or pool and enable WAL for write-heavy use.
Single-item-only API forces pipeline callers into one connection + one transaction + one fsync per record, capping write throughput — add batch variants (executemany within a single transaction).
SQL injection: user-supplied fragment is f-string-interpolated into the LIKE clause instead of parameterized — noted for handoff, detail deferred to critic-security.

Non-atomic check-then-update (TOCTOU) in reserve(): concurrent calls can both read the same stock, pass the check, and oversell — deferred to critic-logic; the atomic fix (UPDATE ... WHERE sku = ? AND stock >= ? with rowcount check) also halves the query count.
Off-by-one: stock > quantity rejects reserving exactly the remaining stock (should be >=) — deferred to critic-logic.
